crossref.publisher: American Association for the Advancement of Science (AAAS)
crossref.abstract:
<jats:p>
            While neutrophil extracellular traps (NETs) have previously been linked to some diabetes-associated complications, such as dysfunctional wound healing, their potential role in diabetic vascular dysfunction has not been studied. Diabetic Akita mice were crossed with either
            <jats:italic>
              Elane
              <jats:sup>−/−</jats:sup>
            </jats:italic>
            or
            <jats:italic>
              Pad4
              <jats:sup>−/−</jats:sup>
            </jats:italic>
            mice to generate NET-deficient diabetic mice. By 24 weeks of age, Akita aortae showed markedly impaired relaxation in response to acetylcholine, indicative of vascular dysfunction. Both Akita-
            <jats:italic>
              Elane
              <jats:sup>−/−</jats:sup>
            </jats:italic>
            mice and Akita-
            <jats:italic>
              Pad4
              <jats:sup>−/−</jats:sup>
            </jats:italic>
            mice had reduced levels of circulating NETs and improved acetylcholine-mediated aortic relaxation. Compared with wild-type aortae, the thromboxane metabolite TXB
            <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
            was roughly 10-fold higher in both intact and endothelium-denuded aortae of Akita mice. In contrast, Akita-
            <jats:italic>
              Elane
              <jats:sup>−/−</jats:sup>
            </jats:italic>
            and Akita-
            <jats:italic>
              Pad4
              <jats:sup>−/−</jats:sup>
            </jats:italic>
            aortae had TXB
            <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
            levels similar to wild type. In summary, inhibition of NETosis by two independent strategies prevented the development of vascular dysfunction in diabetic Akita mice. Thromboxane was up-regulated in the vessel walls of NETosis-competent diabetic mice, suggesting a role for neutrophils in driving the production of this vasoconstrictive and atherogenic prostanoid.
          </jats:p>
title: Inhibition of neutrophil extracellular trap formation alleviates vascular dysfunction in type 1 diabetic mice
